name: Tool Design & MCP Integration
tagline: Write tools the model actually reaches for, control how it picks them, and wire MCP servers with the right auth.
overview: The domain that decides whether your agent can act. It tests tool anatomy and prescriptive descriptions, tool_choice and parallel control, honest error returns, the bash-vs-dedicated-tool judgement call, Anthropic-hosted server-side tools, and the Model Context Protocol — including the trap that hosted MCP servers usually want an OAuth bearer token, not the service's native REST key.
objectives: Author a tool from name, description, and input_schema, writing prescriptive descriptions that state WHEN to call it to lift the should-call rate.
Drive selection with tool_choice (auto/any/tool/none) and force one tool per turn with disable_parallel_tool_use.
Return tool failures honestly with is_error and an informative message so the model can adapt instead of hallucinating success.
Decide bash vs a dedicated tool on reversibility — promote when you must gate, confirm, render, audit, or mark parallel-safe.

Wire MCP and server-side tools correctly: declare a remote server by url and pass the OAuth access token inline as authorization_token (you run the OAuth flow and refresh it) — an OAuth bearer token, not the service's REST API key.

lessons.title: Tool anatomy and prescriptive descriptions
lessons.objective: Build a well-formed tool and write a description that tells the model when to call it.
lessons.body:
## The three parts of a tool

Every tool you hand Claude is three fields:

- **name** — a stable identifier (snake_case), e.g. `get_stock_price`.
- **description** — natural-language guidance the model reads to decide *whether* and *how* to call the tool. This is the highest-leverage field you write.
- **input_schema** — a JSON Schema object describing the parameters, their types, and which are `required`.

```python
tools = [{
    'name': 'get_stock_price',
    'description': 'Get the current trading price for a stock ticker. Call this whenever the user asks about a current or live price, quote, or how a stock is doing today. Do not use it for historical prices.',
    'input_schema': {
        'type': 'object',
        'properties': {
            'ticker': {'type': 'string', 'description': 'Stock ticker symbol, e.g. AAPL'}
        },
        'required': ['ticker']
    }
}]
```

## Prescriptive beats descriptive

Modern Claude models reach for tools **more conservatively** — they prefer to answer directly unless the description makes the case for calling. So write **prescriptive** descriptions that name the *trigger conditions*:

- Weak (descriptive): `Gets a stock price.`
- Strong (prescriptive): `Call this when the user asks about a current price, quote, or how a stock is doing today.`

Stating *when to call* — and when **not** to — measurably lifts the should-call rate and cuts wrong-tool selection. Treat the description as a tiny spec, not a label.

## Schema hygiene

Describe each parameter, mark the truly mandatory ones `required`, and keep the shape flat where you can. If a param has a fixed set of values, use an `enum`. The model fills these from the schema, so an ambiguous schema produces ambiguous arguments.

## Strict tool use

Set `strict: true` on a tool to have the platform **validate** the generated arguments against your schema, so malformed tool inputs are caught rather than passed through. Pair it with tight schemas (types, enums, `required`) for dependable arguments.

knowledgeCheck.stem: Which three fields define a tool you pass to Claude?

options.text: name, description, and input_schema
id, prompt, and output_schema
name, system, and tool_choice
title, body, and parameters_required

knowledgeCheck.explanation: A tool is `name` (identifier), `description` (when/how to use it), and `input_schema` (a JSON Schema of the parameters). `tool_choice` and `system` are request-level settings, not part of a tool object; there is no `output_schema` on a tool definition.

knowledgeCheck.stem: A tool is being under-called — the model answers from memory instead of invoking it. What is the most effective fix to the tool definition?

options.text: Rewrite the description prescriptively to state the conditions under which it should be called
Rename the tool to something shorter
Remove the input_schema so the model has fewer constraints
Add more required parameters to make it look important

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Modern models call tools conservatively, so the description must make the case: name the trigger conditions ('Call this when the user asks about…'). A shorter name doesn't add intent, dropping the schema breaks argument generation, and extra required params make the tool harder to call, not more likely.

knowledgeCheck.stem: What does setting `strict: true` on a tool do?

options.text: Validates the model's generated arguments against the tool's JSON Schema
Forces the model to call that tool on every turn
Disables parallel tool use globally
Makes the tool run on Anthropic's servers instead of yours

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Strict tool use validates generated parameters against your schema so malformed inputs are caught. Forcing a specific tool is `tool_choice`; single-tool-per-turn is `disable_parallel_tool_use`; server execution is a property of server-side/hosted tools, not `strict`.

lessons.title: Controlling selection: tool_choice, parallelism, and honest errors
lessons.objective: Steer which tool runs, force one-at-a-time when needed, and return failures the model can recover from.
lessons.body:
## tool_choice — who decides

The `tool_choice` parameter controls whether and which tool the model uses:

- `{ "type": "auto" }` — the model decides whether to call a tool (the default when tools are present).
- `{ "type": "any" }` — the model **must** call *some* tool, but picks which.
- `{ "type": "tool", "name": "x" }` — the model **must** call tool `x` specifically.
- `{ "type": "none" }` — the model may **not** call any tool this turn.

Use `any`/`tool` to guarantee structured action (e.g. always classify via a tool); use `none` to force a text-only turn even though tools are defined.

## Parallel tool use

By default Claude can emit **multiple** `tool_use` blocks in one turn for independent calls. When ordering matters or a tool has side effects that mustn't overlap, set **`disable_parallel_tool_use: true`** inside `tool_choice` to force **one tool per turn**.

```python
tool_choice = { 'type': 'auto', 'disable_parallel_tool_use': True }
```

## Return errors honestly

When a tool fails, do **not** fabricate a success or return an empty result. Send a `tool_result` with **`is_error: true`** and an informative message, so the model can adapt — retry with different arguments, ask the user, or route around the failure.

```python
{
  'type': 'tool_result',
  'tool_use_id': block.id,
  'content': 'Error: ticker NVDAA not found. Did you mean NVDA?',
  'is_error': True
}
```

A good error string is *actionable*: it states what went wrong and hints at the fix. Swallowing the error (returning `''` or a fake value) makes the model proceed on a false premise — exactly the failure mode honest errors prevent.

## Structured outputs

When you need the *final* answer in a fixed shape (not a tool call), use **structured outputs** via `output_config.format` to constrain the response to a schema. That's distinct from tool use: `tool_choice` shapes *which tool runs*; `output_config.format` shapes the *text the model returns*.

knowledgeCheck.stem: You want Claude to always route a message through your `classify` tool — never answer in free text. Which setting?

options.text: tool_choice = { type: 'tool', name: 'classify' }
tool_choice = { type: 'none' }
tool_choice = { type: 'auto' }
Remove the tool so the model is forced to use it

knowledgeCheck.explanation: `{ type: 'tool', name: 'classify' }` forces that specific tool. `none` forbids tools, `auto` lets the model choose (so it might answer in text), and removing the tool makes it impossible to call.

knowledgeCheck.stem: A `charge_card` tool must never run alongside another tool in the same turn. What do you set?

options.text: disable_parallel_tool_use: true inside tool_choice
strict: true on the tool
tool_choice type 'any'
Lower max_tokens so only one block fits

knowledgeCheck.explanation: `disable_parallel_tool_use: true` forces one tool per turn — the right guard for side-effecting calls. `strict` validates arguments, `any` still allows parallel calls, and `max_tokens` is unrelated to how many tool blocks are emitted.

knowledgeCheck.stem: Your tool call fails because of a bad upstream API response. What should the tool_result contain?

options.text: is_error: true and a clear message describing the failure
An empty string so the model moves on
A plausible fake success value to keep the conversation flowing
Nothing — omit the tool_result for that tool_use_id

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Return `is_error: true` with an informative message so the model can adapt (retry, ask, or route around it). Empty or fabricated results make the model build on a false premise; omitting the tool_result breaks the loop, which requires a result for every tool_use_id.

lessons.title: Bash vs dedicated tools, and Anthropic-hosted server-side tools
lessons.objective: Promote actions to dedicated tools on reversibility, and know what the hosted tools give you.
lessons.body:
## Breadth vs control

A generic **bash** tool gives the model enormous breadth — it can run almost anything — but every call arrives as an **opaque command string**. Your harness can't easily gate it, render it, type-check it, or mark it parallel-safe.

Promote an action from bash to a **dedicated tool** when you need to:

- **Gate or confirm** an irreversible action (delete, deploy, send money).
- **Render custom UI** for the call (a diff view, a confirm dialog).
- **Audit** with a clean, typed record instead of a parsed shell line.
- **Mark it parallel-safe** (or not) so the harness can schedule it correctly.

**Reversibility is the key criterion.** Read-only and easily-undone work is fine over bash; hard-to-reverse actions deserve a typed, interceptable dedicated tool.

## Server-side (Anthropic-hosted) tools

Some tools run on **Anthropic's infrastructure**, not yours. You still declare them in `tools[]`, but you don't execute them — the platform does, and returns the result:

- **web_search** / **web_fetch** — retrieve live web content and return **cited** results.
- **code_execution** — runs code in a **sandboxed container** with data-science libraries. Plain code-exec has **no general internet access** (the sandbox is isolated).
- **memory tool** — persist and recall facts across turns/sessions.

Because these run server-side, a long internal loop can return `stop_reason: pause_turn`; you resume by re-sending the conversation (covered in D1). The distinction to hold: **client-side tools you run; server-side tools Anthropic runs** — both are just entries in `tools[]`.

## Picking between them

Need live web data with citations? Reach for the hosted `web_search`/`web_fetch` rather than hand-rolling a bash `curl`. Need to run untrusted analysis safely? `code_execution`'s sandbox beats giving the model shell on your host. Need a gated, audited, business-specific action? That's a dedicated client-side tool.

knowledgeCheck.stem: What is the key criterion for promoting an action from a generic bash tool to a dedicated tool?

options.text: Reversibility — irreversible actions need a typed tool you can gate, confirm, and audit
Token cost — dedicated tools are always cheaper
Popularity — promote the most frequently called commands
Output length — promote tools that return a lot of text

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Reversibility drives the decision: hard-to-undo actions deserve a typed, interceptable tool so the harness can gate, confirm, render, audit, and schedule them. Bash's opaque command string offers none of that. Token cost and popularity aren't the criterion.

knowledgeCheck.stem: A plain `code_execution` call needs to fetch a page from the public internet. What happens?

options.text: It can't — the code-execution sandbox has no general internet access; use web_fetch/web_search for that
It works normally; the sandbox has full outbound internet
It silently returns the page from Anthropic's cache
It escalates to your host machine to make the request

knowledgeCheck.explanation: The code-execution container is sandboxed and isolated, without general internet access for plain code-exec — that's why it's safe for untrusted analysis. To pull live web content with citations, use the hosted `web_fetch`/`web_search` tools instead.

lessons.title: Model Context Protocol: servers, declaration, and the auth trap
lessons.objective: Declare an MCP server correctly and supply auth the right way — an OAuth bearer token passed inline as authorization_token, not the REST key.
lessons.body:
## What MCP is

The **Model Context Protocol (MCP)** is an open standard for connecting models to external systems. An **MCP server** exposes three kinds of capability:

- **tools** — actions the model can invoke.
- **resources** — data/content the model can read.
- **prompts** — reusable prompt templates the server offers.

Remote servers communicate over **Streamable HTTP / SSE**. The client (your agent) connects to a server and the server's tools become available to Claude.

## Declaring a server via the MCP connector

Using the Messages API MCP connector, you declare a remote MCP server in the `mcp_servers` array by **url**. If the server needs auth, the OAuth access token goes **inline** in that same object as `authorization_token`:

```python
mcp_servers = [{
  'type': 'url',
  'name': 'github',
  'url': 'https://mcp.example.com/github',
  'authorization_token': 'YOUR_OAUTH_ACCESS_TOKEN'  # omit if the server is public
}]
```

The fields are `type` (only `'url'`), `url` (must be `https://`), `name` (a unique id referenced by exactly one `mcp_toolset` in `tools[]`), and the optional `authorization_token`. The connector currently needs the beta header `anthropic-beta: mcp-client-2025-11-20`.

**You run the OAuth flow, not Anthropic.** The docs are explicit: *API consumers are expected to handle the OAuth flow and obtain the access token prior to making the API call, and to refresh the token as needed.* So you complete OAuth against the server (for testing, the MCP inspector's Quick OAuth Flow hands you an `access_token`), then paste that token into `authorization_token`.

## The auth trap (exam favourite)

The single most-missed fact:

> A hosted MCP server usually authenticates with an **OAuth bearer token**, **not** the service's native REST API key. They are different auth systems.

So if a GitHub MCP server expects OAuth, dropping your GitHub *personal access token* / REST API key into `authorization_token` and expecting it to 'just work' is wrong — the server speaks OAuth bearer, and the REST key belongs to a different mechanism. Run the OAuth flow to mint an access token, pass it as `authorization_token`, and refresh it yourself when it expires. Two more don'ts: never put the token in the **url** as a query parameter, and don't confuse this connector-level inline token with Managed Agents' separate credential-vault mechanism.

## Tool search for large libraries

When an agent has **many** tools (dozens of MCP servers, hundreds of tools), loading every schema up front bloats the prompt. **Tool search** lets Claude **discover** the relevant tools on demand: matching tool schemas are **appended** to the context (not swapped in and out). Appending matters because it **preserves the prompt cache** — earlier cached content stays valid, so you get discovery without paying to re-process the whole prompt each turn.

knowledgeCheck.stem: What three kinds of capability does an MCP server expose?

options.text: tools, resources, and prompts
agents, sessions, and environments
models, vaults, and tokens
functions, files, and webhooks

knowledgeCheck.explanation: An MCP server exposes tools (actions), resources (readable data/content), and prompts (reusable templates), over Streamable HTTP/SSE. Agents/sessions/environments are Managed Agents concepts, not MCP server capabilities.

knowledgeCheck.stem: A hosted GitHub MCP server fails to authenticate after you passed your GitHub REST personal-access token as authorization_token. Most likely cause?

options.text: The server expects an OAuth bearer token, which is a different auth system from the REST API key
authorization_token can only hold one credential per request
The url must include the token as a query parameter
MCP servers don't support GitHub

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Hosted MCP servers typically authenticate via OAuth bearer tokens, not the service's native REST API key — they're separate mechanisms. Run the OAuth flow to mint an access token and pass that as `authorization_token`; the REST PAT is the wrong credential type, and you never put tokens in the url.

knowledgeCheck.stem: Why does tool search append matching tool schemas rather than swapping them in and out of the prompt?

options.text: Appending preserves the prompt cache; swapping would invalidate earlier cached context
Appending lets the model call tools without a tool_result
Swapping is impossible over Streamable HTTP
Appending is required to use tool_choice: any

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Schemas are appended so the cached prefix stays valid — you get on-demand discovery for large tool libraries without re-processing the whole prompt each turn. Swapping schemas in/out would change the prefix and bust the cache. It has nothing to do with tool_result or tool_choice.

questions.stem: Which field of a tool definition most directly influences whether Claude decides to call it?

options.text: The description, especially if it states when to call the tool
The name, because longer names rank higher
The number of required parameters
The order of the tool in the tools array

questions.explanation: The description is where the model reads whether and how to use the tool; prescriptive, trigger-stating descriptions raise the should-call rate. Name length, required-param count, and array order are not what drives the decision.

questions.stem: Which description is best for a tool that returns live exchange rates?

options.text: 'Call this when the user asks for a current or live exchange rate between two currencies. Not for historical rates.'
'Exchange rate tool.'
'Returns a number.'
'This is the most powerful currency tool available.'

questions.explanation: The best description is prescriptive: it names the trigger condition (current/live rate, two currencies) and the boundary (not historical). The others are vague labels or marketing — they give the model nothing to decide on.

questions.stem: What does `tool_choice: { type: 'any' }` enforce?

options.text: The model must call some tool, but it chooses which one
The model must call one specific named tool
The model may not call any tool
The model may call at most one tool per conversation

questions.explanation: `any` requires a tool call but leaves the choice of tool to the model. Forcing a specific tool is `type: 'tool', name: …`; forbidding tools is `type: 'none'`; there is no per-conversation cap implied by `any`.

questions.stem: How should a tool report that it could not complete its action?

options.text: Return a tool_result with is_error: true and an informative message
Throw the exception so the whole request fails
Return a normal-looking but fake success value
Return an empty content block

questions.explanation: An honest error — `is_error: true` plus a clear message — lets the model adapt (retry, ask, route around). Crashing the request, faking success, or returning empty all either break the loop or make the model build on a false premise.

questions.stem: An action deletes production records. Why prefer a dedicated `delete_records` tool over letting the agent run `rm`/SQL via a bash tool?

options.text: A dedicated tool gives the harness a typed hook to gate, confirm, and audit an irreversible action
Bash tools can't return errors
Dedicated tools always use fewer tokens
Bash tools are not supported by Claude models

questions.explanation: Irreversible actions need interception — confirmation, validation, audit — which a typed dedicated tool provides and an opaque bash command string does not. Bash can return errors and is supported; token count isn't the reason.

questions.stem: Which statements about Anthropic-hosted server-side tools are correct? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: They are declared in tools[] but executed on Anthropic's infrastructure
web_search and web_fetch return cited results
Plain code_execution has no general internet access
You must execute their calls yourself and return a tool_result

questions.explanation: Server-side tools are listed in `tools[]` but run on Anthropic infra (so you do not execute them or hand-build their tool_result). web_search/web_fetch return cited content, and the code_execution sandbox is isolated without general internet for plain code-exec. Option D describes client-side tools.

questions.stem: Which transport do remote MCP servers use to communicate with a client?

options.text: Streamable HTTP / SSE
Raw TCP sockets only
gRPC bidirectional streams
SMTP

questions.explanation: Remote MCP servers communicate over Streamable HTTP / SSE. The other transports are not the MCP remote transport.

questions.stem: How do you declare a remote MCP server (via the Messages API MCP connector) so Claude can connect to it?

options.text: { type: 'url', name, url, authorization_token } — the OAuth access token inline as authorization_token (omitted only if the server is public)
{ type: 'url', name, url, api_key } with the service's REST key inline
By pasting the OAuth token into the url as a query parameter
By adding the server's tools individually to the tools[] array by hand

questions.explanation: In the `mcp_servers` array you declare the server by `type: 'url'`, `name`, and `url`, and pass the OAuth access token inline as `authorization_token` (leave it off only for a public server). It's an OAuth bearer token, not the service's REST `api_key` (B), and you never put the token in the url (C); the connector surfaces the server's tools for you, so you don't hand-add them (D).

questions.stem: Using the MCP connector, who runs the OAuth flow for an authenticated MCP server, and who keeps the token fresh?

options.text: You do — the API consumer completes the OAuth flow, obtains the access token before the call, passes it as authorization_token, and refreshes it as needed
Anthropic runs OAuth for you and auto-refreshes the token; you supply nothing
You embed the API key in the agent's system prompt and it never expires
Auth isn't needed — MCP servers are always public

questions.explanation: The docs state the API consumer handles the OAuth flow, obtains the access token before making the call, passes it inline as `authorization_token`, and refreshes it as needed — the connector does not run OAuth or auto-refresh for you (B). Secrets in the system prompt persist in history and are the wrong pattern (C), and MCP servers are frequently authenticated, not always public (D).

questions.stem: Which are valid uses of the `tool_choice` parameter? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Force the model to call a specific named tool
Require the model to call some tool but let it pick
Forbid any tool call on this turn
Validate the model's tool arguments against the schema

questions.explanation: `tool_choice` supports `tool` (force a named tool), `any` (must call some tool), `auto` (model decides), and `none` (no tool). Argument validation against the schema is `strict: true`, a separate tool-level setting — not part of tool_choice.

questions.stem: An agent has 300 tools across many MCP servers and the prompt is too large. What does tool search do, and why does it append schemas?

options.text: It discovers relevant tools on demand and appends their schemas, which preserves the prompt cache
It deletes unused tools permanently from the server
It swaps schemas in and out each turn to minimise total tokens
It compresses every schema into a single summary tool

questions.explanation: Tool search surfaces only the relevant tools without loading all schemas up front, appending the matched schemas so the cached prefix stays valid. Swapping schemas in/out would invalidate the cache; it doesn't delete server tools or merge them into one.

questions.stem: Anthropic's guidance calls the tool description 'by far the most important factor in tool performance.' Roughly how much detail does it recommend per description?

options.text: At least 3-4 sentences (more if the tool is complex), covering what it does, when to use it and when not to, and each parameter
One short sentence — the model infers the rest from the name
Exactly one sentence per parameter and nothing about the tool itself
As few words as possible to save prompt tokens

questions.explanation: The docs say to aim for at least 3-4 sentences per description (more if complex), explaining what the tool does, when it should and shouldn't be used, and what each parameter means. A single sentence or a token-minimising label leaves the model guessing; per-parameter-only text omits the crucial when-to-call guidance. Description quality beats saving a few tokens.

questions.stem: You have three separate tools — create_pr, review_pr, and merge_pr — and the model sometimes picks the wrong one. Which refactor does Anthropic recommend?

options.text: Consolidate them into one tool with an 'action' parameter, since fewer, more capable tools reduce selection ambiguity
Split each one further into even more granular tools so each does exactly one thing
Give all three the identical description so the model can't tell them apart
Force the model to always call create_pr with tool_choice

questions.explanation: The docs advise consolidating related operations into a single tool with an action parameter — fewer, more capable tools cut selection ambiguity and are easier for the model to navigate. Splitting further worsens the problem, identical descriptions destroy selectability, and forcing one tool breaks the other two operations.

questions.stem: You aggregate tools from GitHub, Slack, and Jira into one agent. Which tool-naming practice does Anthropic recommend, especially when using tool search?

options.text: Prefix names with the service, e.g. github_list_prs, slack_send_message, so selection stays unambiguous as the library grows
Use the same generic verb (get, do, run) for every tool regardless of service
Give tools random unique IDs so no two names collide
Keep names as short as possible, dropping the service entirely

questions.explanation: Meaningful namespacing — prefixing by service or resource (github_, slack_) — makes selection unambiguous and lets one tool-search query match a whole group. Generic verbs and dropped services create collisions and ambiguity; opaque random IDs are unsearchable and unreadable.

questions.stem: A tool takes a nested, format-sensitive input and the model keeps mis-shaping the arguments. Which tool-definition feature best helps, and what is its constraint?

options.text: Add input_examples with example input objects; each example must itself be valid against the tool's input_schema
Add input_examples, which are exempt from schema validation so you can show edge cases
Set is_error: true so the model retries until the shape is right
Move the tool to the server side so Anthropic fixes the arguments

questions.explanation: input_examples supplies concrete, well-formed calls alongside the schema, which is especially useful for nested or format-sensitive inputs — but every example must validate against input_schema (an invalid example returns a 400). is_error is for reporting failures, not shaping inputs, and server-side execution doesn't repair client argument shape.

questions.stem: input_examples are a great fit for a complex user-defined tool. On which tools are they NOT supported?

options.text: Server-side tools such as web_search and code_execution
Any tool that marks a parameter as required
Tools that use an enum in their schema
Tools that also set strict: true

questions.explanation: input_examples work on user-defined and Anthropic-schema client tools, but not on server tools like web search or code execution — you don't define those schemas. Required params, enums, and strict tool use are all fully compatible with input_examples.

questions.stem: You need a guarantee that the model both calls one of your tools AND that the arguments strictly match your schema. Which combination delivers both?

options.text: tool_choice: { type: 'any' } together with strict: true on the tool definitions
tool_choice: { type: 'auto' } alone
strict: true alone, with no tool_choice set
tool_choice: { type: 'none' } together with strict: true

questions.explanation: Combining tool_choice 'any' (must call some tool) with strict: true (schema-validated arguments) guarantees both a tool call and conformant inputs. 'auto' may answer in text; 'strict' alone doesn't force a call; 'none' forbids all tools, so nothing runs.

questions.stem: Your request uses extended thinking. You try tool_choice: { type: 'tool', name: 'classify' } to force that tool and get a 400 error. Why?

options.text: With extended thinking, forced tool choice ('any' and 'tool') isn't supported — only 'auto' and 'none' are compatible
Extended thinking disables all tool use, so no tool_choice value is valid
The tool name must be omitted when extended thinking is on
Extended thinking requires strict: true before any tool_choice works

questions.explanation: When extended thinking is enabled, tool_choice 'any' and 'tool' are not supported and error out; only 'auto' (default) and 'none' are compatible. Extended thinking does allow tool use (via 'auto'), the name isn't the issue, and strict is unrelated to this restriction.

questions.stem: You force a tool with tool_choice 'any' or 'tool' but also want the model to say a sentence to the user before the tool_use block. What actually happens, and what's the fix?

options.text: 'any'/'tool' prefill the assistant turn so no natural-language text precedes the tool_use; use 'auto' plus a user-message instruction to get commentary
The model always adds a sentence regardless of tool_choice; no change is needed
Set disable_parallel_tool_use: true to re-enable the preamble
Lower max_tokens so the model summarises before calling the tool

questions.explanation: With 'any' or 'tool', the API prefills the assistant message to force a tool call, so the model emits no natural-language explanation first — even if asked. To get commentary, use 'auto' (the default) and instruct it in a user message (e.g. 'Use the get_weather tool in your response'). disable_parallel_tool_use only limits concurrency; max_tokens doesn't restore the preamble.

questions.stem: You need the model's FINAL answer as a fixed JSON object for a downstream service — not a tool call. Which mechanism fits, and how does it relate to strict tool use?

options.text: Structured outputs via output_config.format (type: json_schema) constrain the response text; strict tool use validates tool-call inputs — different problems that can work together
strict: true on a tool, because it also forces the final text into your schema
tool_choice: 'any', which formats the assistant's text as JSON
disable_parallel_tool_use, which serialises output into a single JSON block

questions.explanation: Structured outputs (output_config.format with a json_schema) constrain what the model says — the final response is guaranteed valid JSON for your schema. Strict tool use instead validates how the model calls your functions (tool inputs). They solve different problems and compose. tool_choice and disable_parallel_tool_use steer tool calling, not the shape of the final text.

questions.stem: The tool search tool ships in two variants. What is the difference in how Claude queries them?

options.text: The regex variant has Claude write Python re.search() patterns; the BM25 variant has Claude search with natural-language queries
The regex variant uses natural language; the BM25 variant uses SQL WHERE clauses
Both variants require you to pre-compute embeddings for every tool
The variants differ only in price; the query format is identical

questions.explanation: With the regex tool search variant Claude constructs Python re.search() patterns (case-insensitive, up to 200 chars); with the BM25 variant it issues natural-language queries (up to 500 chars). Neither built-in variant needs you to pre-compute embeddings (that's the custom client-side approach), and the query format genuinely differs.

questions.stem: When is enabling the tool search tool the right call, and how do you configure the toolset? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Reach for it once you have roughly 10+ tools or definitions exceeding ~10k tokens, or when selection accuracy drops as the set grows
Set defer_loading: true on the tools that shouldn't load up front, keeping your 3-5 most-used tools non-deferred
At least one tool (normally the tool search tool itself) must stay non-deferred, or the request 400s
Set defer_loading: true on the tool search tool too, so even it loads on demand

questions.explanation: Tool search fits when you have ~10+ tools, >~10k tokens of definitions, or degrading selection accuracy (which starts once you pass ~30-50 tools). You mark deferred tools with defer_loading: true while keeping the 3-5 hottest tools non-deferred, and at least one tool must remain non-deferred (normally the search tool) or the API returns a 400 — so never defer the tool search tool itself.

questions.stem: Using the Messages API MCP connector (not a self-hosted MCP client), which statement is correct about what it supports?

options.text: Only MCP tool calls are supported through the connector, and the server must be publicly reachable over HTTP (Streamable HTTP or SSE) — local stdio servers can't connect directly
The connector exposes tools, resources, and prompts equally, and connects to local stdio servers
The connector requires a gRPC endpoint and does not support SSE
The connector only works with servers hosted by Anthropic

questions.explanation: Through the Messages API MCP connector, only tool calls are currently supported, and the server must be publicly exposed over HTTP — both Streamable HTTP and SSE transports work, but local STDIO servers cannot be connected directly (use a client-side MCP client for stdio, prompts, or resources). It is not gRPC-only and is not limited to Anthropic-hosted servers.

questions.stem: You connect a calendar MCP server via the connector but must stop the agent from ever calling its destructive tools (e.g. delete_all_events). What is the cleanest way?

options.text: In the mcp_toolset, denylist those tools by setting enabled: false for them in configs (or allowlist only the safe tools via default_config.enabled: false + per-tool enables)
Delete the tools from the remote MCP server's source code
Add is_error: true to those tools so calls always fail
Put the destructive tool names in the mcp_servers URL as a blocklist query string

questions.explanation: The mcp_toolset in the tools array controls which of a server's tools are exposed: denylist by setting enabled: false in configs, or allowlist by defaulting enabled: false and enabling only the safe tools — exactly what the docs recommend for read-only or confirm-first assistants. You don't edit the server's code, is_error is a runtime result not a config, and the URL carries no tool blocklist.

scenarios.title: Tooling a payments support agent
scenarios.tagline: Description, gating, parallelism, and honest errors on a money-moving toolset.
scenarios.context: You're adding tools to a support agent that can look up orders and issue refunds. Refunds are irreversible. The agent keeps refunding when it should only quote a price, and a flaky payment API sometimes 500s. You own the tool definitions.

steps.prompt: The agent over-calls `issue_refund` for questions that only need a price quote. First fix?

choices.text: Rewrite the refund tool's description prescriptively — 'Call only when the user explicitly requests a refund of a specific order' — and give a separate get_price tool a clear when-to-call line
choices.rationale: The description is the lever: stating the exact trigger (and boundary) for each tool fixes wrong-tool selection at the source, which is what conservative modern models read.

choices.text: Delete the refund tool so it can never fire by mistake
choices.rationale: That removes a required capability to dodge a description problem; the agent can no longer do its job.

choices.text: Set tool_choice to 'any' so the model is forced to pick a tool every turn
choices.rationale: It forces a tool but doesn't fix which one — the model may still pick refund over price. It also forbids plain text answers. Fix the descriptions first.

steps.prompt: How should the refund capability itself be shaped, given refunds are irreversible?

choices.text: A dedicated issue_refund tool the harness can gate with confirmation and audit
choices.rationale: Reversibility is the criterion: an irreversible action belongs in a typed, interceptable tool with a confirmation gate and a clean audit record.

choices.text: A generic bash tool so the agent can curl the refund endpoint
choices.rationale: Bash hands the harness an opaque command string — no gate, no typed validation, no clean audit. Exactly the wrong shape for an irreversible action.

choices.text: A dedicated tool, but auto-approved for every amount to cut latency
choices.rationale: Right tool shape, weak policy: skipping confirmation on an irreversible money action throws away the main benefit of promoting it.

steps.prompt: A refund and an unrelated order-lookup must not run in the same turn so the refund can be confirmed in isolation. What do you set?

choices.text: disable_parallel_tool_use: true so the model emits one tool call per turn
choices.rationale: Forcing one tool per turn lets you intercept and confirm the side-effecting refund cleanly, without a parallel lookup muddying the turn.

choices.text: strict: true on both tools
choices.rationale: strict validates arguments against the schema; it does nothing about how many tools run in a turn.

choices.text: Leave parallelism on and just hope the model serialises them
choices.rationale: Default parallelism may emit both at once; relying on luck for a money action is fragile. Disable parallel use to make it deterministic.

steps.prompt: The payment API returns a 500 mid-refund. What does the tool return to the model?

choices.text: A tool_result with is_error: true and a message like 'Payment gateway returned 500; refund not applied, safe to retry'
choices.rationale: An honest, actionable error lets the model retry or escalate instead of assuming the refund went through — critical when the action is irreversible.

choices.text: A fake success so the conversation stays smooth
choices.rationale: Faking success on a failed refund is dangerous: the model (and user) believe money moved when it didn't.

choices.text: An empty tool_result and let the model infer something went wrong
choices.rationale: Better than a fake success, but an empty result gives the model nothing to act on; an informative is_error message is far stronger.

scenarios.title: Connecting a managed agent to a GitHub MCP server
scenarios.tagline: Declaration, auth, hosted vs custom tools, and scaling the tool library.
scenarios.context: A Managed Agent must use a remote GitHub MCP server to read issues and open PRs, plus pull live release notes from the web. You're wiring the connections and auth, and the agent will eventually have many MCP servers attached.

steps.prompt: How do you declare the GitHub MCP server through the connector?

choices.text: { type: 'url', name: 'github', url: '…', authorization_token: <OAuth access token> } in mcp_servers, with a matching mcp_toolset in tools[]
choices.rationale: The documented pattern: declare the server by url and pass the OAuth access token inline as authorization_token (GitHub's server needs auth), then reference it from an mcp_toolset.

choices.text: Put the GitHub REST token inside the server object as api_key
choices.rationale: There is no api_key field, and the REST token is the wrong credential type — the server wants an OAuth bearer token in authorization_token.

choices.text: Skip MCP and re-implement each GitHub action as a hand-written bash curl tool
choices.rationale: It works but throws away MCP's ready-made, maintained tools/resources/prompts and adds a pile of opaque bash to gate yourself.

steps.prompt: The GitHub MCP server expects OAuth. How do you supply that auth?

choices.text: Run the OAuth flow yourself, obtain an access token, and pass it inline as authorization_token — refreshing it yourself as it expires
choices.rationale: The docs say the API consumer handles the OAuth flow, obtains the access token before the call, passes it as authorization_token, and refreshes it as needed. The connector does not run OAuth or refresh for you.

choices.text: Pass your GitHub REST personal-access token as authorization_token and assume it works
choices.rationale: Hosted MCP usually wants an OAuth bearer token, not the service's REST API key — different auth systems. The PAT is the wrong credential type.

choices.text: Mint a long-lived token once and never refresh it
choices.rationale: Technically it authenticates until it expires, but long-lived tokens are a security liability and the flow still needs you to refresh — plan for token rotation instead.

steps.prompt: The agent also needs current release notes from the public web. Best tool choice?

choices.text: The hosted web_fetch / web_search tools, which return cited results
choices.rationale: Live web data with citations is exactly what the hosted web tools provide, run on Anthropic infra and declared in tools[].

choices.text: A bash tool running curl against the release page
choices.rationale: It can fetch bytes but gives you no citations, no managed sandbox, and an opaque command to gate. The hosted tool is the cleaner fit.

choices.text: code_execution that downloads the page in Python
choices.rationale: Plain code_execution is sandboxed with no general internet access, so the fetch fails. Use web_fetch for live web content.

steps.prompt: Over time the agent accumulates many MCP servers and hundreds of tools, bloating the prompt. What do you enable?

choices.text: Tool search, so Claude discovers relevant tools on demand and their schemas are appended
choices.rationale: Tool search avoids loading every schema up front; appending the matched schemas keeps the prompt cache valid while still surfacing the right tools.

choices.text: Manually delete servers until only a handful remain
choices.rationale: It shrinks the prompt but at the cost of capability; tool search keeps the breadth without the upfront token bloat.

choices.text: Swap tool schemas in and out of the prompt each turn to save tokens
choices.rationale: Swapping changes the cached prefix and busts the prompt cache; tool search deliberately appends instead, for exactly that reason.

labs.title: Design a tool and prove the description drives the call
labs.goal: Write a tool with a prescriptive description, then steer and validate its use.
labs.steps:
## What you'll build

A single `get_weather` tool, plus a small harness that shows how the description and tool_choice change behaviour.

## Steps

1. Define `get_weather` with an `input_schema` requiring `location` (string), and a deliberately weak description ('Gets the weather.').
2. Ask 'What should I wear in Dublin today?' and observe whether the model calls the tool or answers from memory.
3. Rewrite the description prescriptively: 'Call this whenever the user asks about current weather, temperature, or what to wear for a specific place.' Re-run and compare the should-call rate.
4. Force the call with `tool_choice = { type: 'tool', name: 'get_weather' }`; then forbid it with `{ type: 'none' }` and confirm a text-only answer.
5. Add `strict: true` and send a malformed expectation (e.g. omit `location`) to see argument validation.
6. Make the tool return `is_error: true` with an informative message for an unknown location, and watch the model adapt.

## Why it matters

This is the core craft of D2: the description is a spec, tool_choice is the steering wheel, and honest errors keep the model grounded.
checklist: Weak vs prescriptive description visibly changes whether the tool is called
tool_choice 'tool' forces the call; 'none' produces a text-only turn
strict: true rejects arguments that violate the schema
An is_error result with a clear message lets the model recover

labs.title: Wire an MCP server with an inline OAuth token
labs.goal: Declare a remote MCP server through the connector and authenticate it the right way — an OAuth access token passed inline as authorization_token.
labs.steps:
## Steps

1. Set the connector beta header `anthropic-beta: mcp-client-2025-11-20` and declare a remote MCP server in `mcp_servers` as `{ type: 'url', name, url }` (start with a public server — no token yet).
2. Run the **OAuth** flow against an authenticated server to obtain an **access token** (for testing, `npx @modelcontextprotocol/inspector` → Quick OAuth Flow gives you an `access_token`) — an OAuth bearer token, not the service's REST API key.
3. Paste that token into the server object as `authorization_token`, and add a matching `mcp_toolset` entry in `tools[]`; call one tool through Claude.
4. Deliberately swap in the REST API key instead of the OAuth access token and observe the auth failure — internalise that they are different systems.
5. Let the token expire (or revoke it) and confirm **you** must obtain a fresh one and re-pass it — the connector does not refresh it for you.
6. Attach several more servers and enable **tool search**; confirm relevant tool schemas are appended on demand and the prompt cache is preserved.

## Checkpoint

If you ever pasted the token into the server `url` as a query parameter, stop — the OAuth access token belongs in the `authorization_token` field of the `mcp_servers` object, and you run and refresh the OAuth flow yourself.
checklist: Connector beta header set; server declared by { type: 'url', name, url }
OAuth access token passed inline as authorization_token (an OAuth bearer token, not the REST API key)
You obtain and refresh the token yourself — the connector does not auto-refresh it
A matching mcp_toolset lets Claude call a tool from the server
Tool search appends schemas on demand without busting the prompt cache

resources.title: Tool use overview (Anthropic)
resources.annotation: Extract the tool object shape (name/description/input_schema), tool_choice options, disable_parallel_tool_use, and the is_error tool_result. The backbone of D2.

resources.title: Model Context Protocol — spec & concepts
resources.annotation: Read the transport (Streamable HTTP/SSE) and the tools/resources/prompts sections. Extract what a server exposes and how a client connects.

resources.title: MCP connector / connecting to remote servers
resources.annotation: Extract how mcp_servers is declared ({type:'url', name, url, authorization_token}) with the OAuth access token passed inline and the consumer running/refreshing the OAuth flow — the source for the OAuth-vs-REST-key distinction.

resources.title: Server-side tools: web search, code execution, memory
resources.annotation: Extract which tools run on Anthropic infra, that web_search/web_fetch return citations, and that plain code_execution has no general internet access.

resources.title: Define tools — descriptions, examples, and forcing tool use (Anthropic)
resources.annotation: Source for description best practices (3-4+ sentences, when-to-use/when-not, consolidation, namespacing), input_examples (schema-validated, not for server tools), and the four tool_choice options incl. the extended-thinking and prefill caveats.

resources.title: Tool search tool — scaling to thousands of tools (Anthropic)
resources.annotation: Source for the regex vs BM25 variants, defer_loading (at least one tool non-deferred), when to enable (~10+ tools / >10k tokens / accuracy drop past ~30-50), and that appended tool_reference blocks preserve the prompt cache.
